sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dizziness refers to a range of sensations including feeling light-headed, faint, woozy, unsteady or as if the surroundings are spinning. It is often caused by disruptions in the body’s balance system, which involves the inner ear, eyes, brain and nerves. In the prehospital setting, dizziness can be a symptom of a wide variety of conditions, from benign disorders to life-threatening emergencies. Common causes include dehydration, low blood pressure, inner ear disorders, medication side effects, and stress.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sensations of spinning and visual disturbances
- Loss of balance, unsteady, weak
- Disorientation, confusion and difficulty concentrating
- Pallor and clamminess
- Nausea and/or vomiting
- Nystagmus

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Patients presenting with true vertigo should be managed under the vertigo guideline
- Dizziness can appear minor but may reflect stroke, dysrhythmia, ACS, sepsis, dehydration, anaemia, hypoglycaemia, medication effect, toxic exposure, or heat illness
- A sudden debilitating onset with neurological symptoms should raise concern for posterior circulation stroke
- Sudden dizziness with hearing loss, tinnitus, severe headache, chest pain, syncope, palpitations, trauma, or significant vomiting warrants a low threshold for transport and escalation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient safely, especially if standing worsens symptoms
- Administer oxygen only if indicated
- Record vital signs, including pulse, blood pressure, blood glucose, temperature, and postural observations where safe and relevant
- Perform focused neurological assessment, including FAST where appropriate, and assess hydration, ear symptoms, and cardiac symptoms
- Consider urinalysis only where it is clinically relevant and will alter immediate management

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG where cardiac, syncope-related, or unexplained dizziness is suspected
- Manage dysrhythmia or other identified causes in line with the relevant condition-specific guideline
- Consider IV access if dehydration, persistent vomiting, abnormal vitals, or another serious diagnosis is suspected
- Consider point-of-care blood testing where available and clinically useful
- Consider fluid therapy only where hypovolaemia or dehydration is clinically suspected
- Consider antiemetic therapy if vomiting is prominent and not better explained by another urgent cause

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Some pre-hospital procedures such as injections may precipitate dizziness in susceptible patients. Patient position should be considered to avoid potential injury
